Vulnerable Road User Research, , , provided that:Pub. L. 117–58, div. A, title I, § 11122135 Stat. 497Nov. 15, 2021“(a).—DefinitionsIn this subsection [probably means “this section”]:“(1).—AdministratorThe term ‘Administrator’ means the Secretary [of Transportation], acting through the Administrator of the Federal Highway Administration.“(2).—Vulnerable road userThe term ‘vulnerable road user’ has the meaning given the term in , United States Code.section 148(a) of title 23“(b).—Establishment of Research PlanThe Administrator shall establish a research plan to prioritize research on roadway designs, the development of safety countermeasures to minimize fatalities and serious injuries to vulnerable road users, and the promotion of bicycling and walking, including research relating to—“(1)roadway safety improvements, including traffic calming techniques and vulnerable road user accommodations appropriate in a suburban arterial context;“(2)the impacts of traffic speeds, and access to low-traffic stress corridors, on safety and rates of bicycling and walking;“(3)tools to evaluate the impact of transportation improvements on projected rates and safety of bicycling and walking; and“(4)other research areas to be determined by the Administrator.“(c).—Vulnerable Road User AssessmentsThe Administrator shall—“(1)review each vulnerable road user safety assessment submitted by a State under section 148() of title 23, United States Code, and other relevant sources of data to determine what, if any, standard definitions and methods should be developed through guidance to enable a State to collect pedestrian injury and fatality data; andl“(2)in the first progress update under subsection (d)(2), provide—“(A)the results of the determination described in paragraph (1); and“(B)the recommendations of the Secretary with respect to the collection and reporting of data on the safety of vulnerable road users.“(d)Submission; Publication.—“(1).—Submission of planNot later than 180 days after the date of enactment of this Act [], the Administrator shall submit to the Committee on Environment and Public Works of the Senate and the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure of the House of Representatives the research plan described in subsection (b).Nov. 15, 2021“(2).—Progress updatesNot later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this Act, and biannually thereafter, the Administrator shall submit to the Committees described in paragraph (1)—“(A)updates on the progress and findings of the research conducted pursuant to the plan described in subsection (b); and“(B)in the first submission under this paragraph, the results and recommendations described in subsection (c)(2).”

Stopping Threats on Pedestrians, , , provided that:Pub. L. 117–58, div. A, title I, § 11502135 Stat. 578Nov. 15, 2021“(a).—Definition of Bollard Installation ProjectIn this section, the term ‘bollard installation project’ means a project to install raised concrete or metal posts on a sidewalk adjacent to a roadway that are designed to slow or stop a motor vehicle.“(b).—EstablishmentNot later than 1 year after the date of enactment of this Act [] and subject to the availability of appropriations, the Secretary [of Transportation] shall establish and carry out a competitive grant pilot program to provide assistance to State departments of transportation and local government entities for bollard installation projects designed to prevent pedestrian injuries and acts of terrorism in areas used by large numbers of pedestrians.Nov. 15, 2021“(c).—ApplicationTo be eligible to receive a grant under this section, a State department of transportation or local government entity shall submit to the Secretary an application at such time, in such form, and containing such information as the Secretary determines to be appropriate, which shall include, at a minimum—“(1)a description of the proposed bollard installation project to be carried out;“(2)a description of the pedestrian injury or terrorism risks with respect to the proposed installation area; and“(3)an analysis of how the proposed bollard installation project will mitigate those risks.“(d).—Use of FundsA recipient of a grant under this section may only use the grant funds for a bollard installation project.“(e).—Federal ShareThe Federal share of the costs of a bollard installation project carried out with a grant under this section may be up to 100 percent.“(f).—Authorization of AppropriationsThere is authorized to be appropriated to the Secretary to carry out this section $5,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2022 through 2026.“(g).—Treatment of ProjectsNotwithstanding any other provision of law, a project assisted under this section shall be treated as a project on a Federal-aid highway under chapter 1 of title 23, United States Code.”

Study of High-risk Rural Roads Best Practices, , , provided that:Pub. L. 117–58, div. A, title I, § 11111(b)135 Stat. 478Nov. 15, 2021“(1).—StudyNot later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this Act [], the Secretary shall update the study under section 1112(b)(1) of MAP–21 ( note; ).Nov. 15, 202123 U.S.C. 148Public Law 112–141“(2).—Publication of reportNot later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary shall publish on the website of the Department of Transportation an update to the report described in section 1112(b)(2) of MAP–21 ( note; ).23 U.S.C. 148Public Law 112–141“(3).—Best practices manualNot later than 180 days after the date on which the report is published under paragraph (2), the Secretary shall update the best practices manual described in section 1112(b)(3) of MAP–21 ( note; ).”23 U.S.C. 148Public Law 112–141, , , provided that:Pub. L. 112–141, div. A, title I, § 1112(b)126 Stat. 459July 6, 2012“(1)Study.—“(A).—In generalThe Secretary [of Transportation] shall conduct a study of the best practices for implementing cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements on high-risk rural roads.“(B).—MethodologyIn carrying out the study, the Secretary shall—“(i)conduct a thorough literature review;“(ii)survey current practices of State departments of transportation; and“(iii)survey current practices of local units of government, as appropriate.“(C).—ConsultationIn carrying out the study, the Secretary shall consult with—“(i)State departments of transportation;“(ii)county engineers and public works professionals;“(iii)appropriate local officials; and“(iv)appropriate private sector experts in the field of roadway safety infrastructure.“(2)Report.—“(A).—In generalNot later than 1 year after the date of enactment of this Act [see section 3(a), (b) of , set out as Effective and Termination Dates of 2012 Amendment notes under ], the Secretary shall submit to the Committee on Environment and Public Works of the Senate and the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure of the House of Representatives a report on the results of the study.Pub. L. 112–141section 101 of this title“(B).—ContentsThe report shall include—“(i)a summary of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements;“(ii)a summary of the latest research on the financial savings and reduction in fatalities and serious bodily injury crashes from the implementation of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements; and“(iii)recommendations for State and local governments on best practice methods to install cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure on high-risk rural roads.“(3)Manual.—“(A).—DevelopmentBased on the results of the study under paragraph (2), the Secretary, in consultation with the individuals and entities described in paragraph (1)(C), shall develop a best practices manual to support Federal, State, and local efforts to reduce fatalities and serious bodily injury crashes on high-risk rural roads through the use of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements.“(B).—AvailabilityThe manual shall be made available to State and local governments not later than 180 days after the date of submission of the report under paragraph (2).“(C).—ContentsThe manual shall include, at a minimum, a list of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements and best practices on the installation of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements on high-risk rural roads.“(D).—Use of manualUse of the manual shall be voluntary and the manual shall not establish any binding standards or legal duties on State or local governments, or any other person.”

Transition, formerly § 1401(e), , , renumbered § 1401(d) by , , , provided for different methods of obligating funds to States for highway safety improvement programs both before and after the second fiscal year beginning .Pub. A, title I, § 11111(b)135 Stat. 478Nov. 15, 2021“(1).—StudyNot later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this Act [], the Secretary shall update the study under section 1112(b)(1) of MAP–21 ( note; ).Nov. 15, 202123 U.S.C. 148Public Law 112–141“(2).—Publication of reportNot later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary shall publish on the website of the Department of Transportation an update to the report described in section 1112(b)(2) of MAP–21 ( note; ).23 U.S.C. 148Public Law 112–141“(3).—Best practices manualNot later than 180 days after the date on which the report is published under paragraph (2), the Secretary shall update the best practices manual described in section 1112(b)(3) of MAP–21 ( note; ).”23 U.S.C. 148Public Law 112–141, , , provided that:Pub. L. 112–141, div. A, title I, § 1112(b)126 Stat. 459July 6, 2012“(1)Study.—“(A).—In generalThe Secretary [of Transportation] shall conduct a study of the best practices for implementing cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements on high-risk rural roads.“(B).—MethodologyIn carrying out the study, the Secretary shall—“(i)conduct a thorough literature review;“(ii)survey current practices of State departments of transportation; and“(iii)survey current practices of local units of government, as appropriate.“(C).—ConsultationIn carrying out the study, the Secretary shall consult with—“(i)State departments of transportation;“(ii)county engineers and public works professionals;“(iii)appropriate local officials; and“(iv)appropriate private sector experts in the field of roadway safety infrastructure.“(2)Report.—“(A).—In generalNot later than 1 year after the date of enactment of this Act [see section 3(a), (b) of , set out as Effective and Termination Dates of 2012 Amendment notes under ], the Secretary shall submit to the Committee on Environment and Public Works of the Senate and the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure of the House of Representatives a report on the results of the study.Pub. L. 112–141section 101 of this title“(B).—ContentsThe report shall include—“(i)a summary of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements;“(ii)a summary of the latest research on the financial savings and reduction in fatalities and serious bodily injury crashes from the implementation of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements; and“(iii)recommendations for State and local governments on best practice methods to install cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure on high-risk rural roads.“(3)Manual.—“(A).—DevelopmentBased on the results of the study under paragraph (2), the Secretary, in consultation with the individuals and entities described in paragraph (1)(C), shall develop a best practices manual to support Federal, State, and local efforts to reduce fatalities and serious bodily injury crashes on high-risk rural roads through the use of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements.“(B).—AvailabilityThe manual shall be made available to State and local governments not later than 180 days after the date of submission of the report under paragraph (2).“(C).—ContentsThe manual shall include, at a minimum, a list of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements and best practices on the installation of cost-effective roadway safety infrastructure improvements on high-risk rural roads.“(D).—Use of manualUse of the manual shall be voluntary and the manual shall not establish any binding standards or legal duties on State or local governments, or any other person.”

Transition, formerly § 1401(e), , , renumbered § 1401(d) by , , , provided for different methods of obligating funds to States for highway safety improvement programs both before and after the second fiscal year beginning .Pub. L. 109–59, title I, § 1401(d)119 Stat. 1227Pub. L. 110–244, title I, § 101(s)(1)122 Stat. 1577Aug. 10, 2005June 6, 2008Aug. 10, 2005

children.heading: .—High risk rural road
children.label: 1 .—High risk rural road
children.text: The term “high risk rural road” means any roadway functionally classified as a rural major or minor collector or a rural local road with significant safety risks, as defined by a State in accordance with an updated State strategic highway safety plan.

children.heading: .—Highway basemap
children.label: 2 .—Highway basemap
children.text: The term “highway basemap” means a representation of all public roads that can be used to geolocate attribute data on a roadway.

children.heading: .—Highway safety improvement program
children.label: 3 .—Highway safety improvement program
children.text: The term “highway safety improvement program” means projects, activities, plans, and reports carried out under this section.

children.label: i
children.text: correct or improve a hazardous road location or feature; or

children.label: ii
children.text: address a highway safety problem.

children.label: i
children.text: An intersection safety improvement that provides for the safety of all road users, as appropriate, including a multimodal roundabout.

children.label: ii
children.text: Pavement and shoulder widening (including addition of a passing lane to remedy an unsafe condition).

children.label: iii
children.text: Installation of rumble strips or another warning device, if the rumble strips or other warning devices do not adversely affect the safety or mobility of bicyclists and pedestrians, including persons with disabilities.

children.label: iv
children.text: Installation of a skid-resistant surface at an intersection or other location with a high frequency of crashes.

children.label: v
children.text: An improvement for pedestrian or bicyclist safety or safety of persons with disabilities.

children.label: vi
children.text: Construction and improvement of a railway-highway grade crossing safety feature, including installation of protective devices or a grade separation project.

children.label: vii
children.text: The conduct of a model traffic enforcement activity at a railway-highway crossing.

children.label: viii
children.text: Construction or installation of features, measures, and road designs to calm traffic and reduce vehicle speeds.

children.label: ix
children.text: Elimination of a roadside hazard.

children.label: x
children.text: Installation, replacement, and other improvement of highway signage and pavement markings, or a project to maintain minimum levels of retroreflectivity, that addresses a highway safety problem consistent with a State strategic highway safety plan.

children.label: xi
children.text: Installation of a priority control system for emergency vehicles at signalized intersections.

children.label: xii
children.text: Installation of a traffic control or other warning device at a location with high crash potential.

children.label: xiii
children.text: Transportation safety planning.

children.label: xiv
children.text: Collection, analysis, and improvement of safety data.

children.label: xv
children.text: Planning integrated interoperable emergency communications equipment, operational activities, or traffic enforcement activities (including police assistance) relating to work zone safety.

children.label: xvi
children.text: Installation of guardrails, barriers (including barriers between construction work zones and traffic lanes for the safety of road users and workers), and crash attenuators.

children.label: xvii
children.text: The addition or retrofitting of structures or other measures to eliminate or reduce crashes involving vehicles and wildlife.

children.label: xviii
children.text: Installation of yellow-green signs and signals at pedestrian and bicycle crossings and in school zones.

children.label: xix
children.text: Construction and operational improvements on high risk rural roads.

children.label: xx
children.text: Geometric improvements to a road for safety purposes that improve safety.

children.label: xxi
children.text: A road safety audit.

children.label: xxii
children.text: Roadway safety infrastructure improvements consistent with the recommendations included in the publication of the Federal Highway Administration entitled “Highway Design Handbook for Older Drivers and Pedestrians” (FHWA–RD–01–103), dated May 2001 or as subsequently revised and updated.

children.label: xxiii
children.text: Truck parking facilities eligible for funding under section 1401 of the MAP–21.

children.label: xxiv
children.text: Systemic safety improvements.

children.label: xxv
children.text: Installation of vehicle-to-infrastructure communication equipment.

children.label: xxvi
children.text: Installation or upgrades of traffic control devices for pedestrians and bicyclists, including pedestrian hybrid beacons and the addition of bicycle movement phases to traffic signals.

children.label: xxvii
children.text: Roadway improvements that provide separation between pedestrians and motor vehicles or between bicyclists and motor vehicles, including medians, pedestrian crossing islands, protected bike lanes, and protected intersection features.

children.label: xxviii
children.text: A pedestrian security feature designed to slow or stop a motor vehicle.

children.label: xxix
children.text: A physical infrastructure safety project not described in clauses (i) through (xxviii).

children.heading: .—Model inventory of roadway elements
children.label: 5 .—Model inventory of roadway elements
children.text: The term “model inventory of roadway elements” means the listing and standardized coding by the Federal Highway Administration of roadway and traffic data elements critical to safety management, analysis, and decisionmaking.

children.heading: .—Project to maintain minimum levels of retroreflectivity
children.label: 6 .—Project to maintain minimum levels of retroreflectivity
children.text: The term “project to maintain minimum levels of retroreflectivity” means a project that is designed to maintain a highway sign or pavement marking retroreflectivity at or above the minimum levels prescribed in Federal or State regulations.

children.heading: .—Road safety audit
children.label: 7 .—Road safety audit
children.text: The term “road safety audit” means a formal safety performance examination of an existing or future road or intersection by an independent multidisciplinary audit team.

children.heading: .—Road users
children.label: 8 .—Road users
children.text: The term “road user” means a motorist, passenger, public transportation operator or user, truck driver, bicyclist, motorcyclist, or pedestrian, including a person with disabilities.

children.label: A
children.text: that emphasizes minimizing the risk of injury or fatality to road users; and

children.label: i
children.text: takes into consideration the possibility and likelihood of human error;

children.label: ii
children.text: accommodates human injury tolerance by taking into consideration likely accident types, resulting impact forces, and the ability of the human body to withstand impact forces; and

children.label: iii
children.text: takes into consideration vulnerable road users.

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: The term “safety data” means crash, roadway, and traffic data on a public road.

children.heading: .—Inclusion
children.label: B .—Inclusion
children.text: The term “safety data” includes, in the case of a railway-highway grade crossing, the characteristics of highway and train traffic, licensing, and vehicle data.

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: The term “specified safety project” means a project carried out for the purpose of safety under any other section of this title that is consistent with the State strategic highway safety plan.

children.label: i
children.text: promotes public awareness and informs the public regarding highway safety matters (including safety for motorcyclists, bicyclists, pedestrians, individuals with disabilities, and other road users);

children.label: ii
children.text: facilitates enforcement of traffic safety laws;

children.label: iii
children.text: provides infrastructure and infrastructure-related equipment to support emergency services;

children.label: iv
children.text: conducts safety-related research to evaluate experimental safety countermeasures or equipment; or

children.label: v
children.text: supports safe routes to school noninfrastructure-related activities described in section 208(g)(2).

children.heading: .—State highway safety improvement program
children.label: 12 .—State highway safety improvement program
children.text: The term “State highway safety improvement program” means a program of highway safety improvement projects, activities, plans and reports carried out as part of the Statewide transportation improvement program under section 135(g).

children.label: i
children.text: a highway safety representative of the Governor of the State;

children.label: ii
children.text: regional transportation planning organizations and metropolitan planning organizations, if any;

children.label: iii
children.text: representatives of major modes of transportation;

children.label: iv
children.text: State and local traffic enforcement officials;

children.label: v
children.text: a highway-rail grade crossing safety representative of the Governor of the State;

children.label: vi
children.text: representatives conducting a motor carrier safety program under section 31102, 31106, or 31309 of title 49;

children.label: vii
children.text: motor vehicle administration agencies;

children.label: viii
children.text: county transportation officials;

children.label: ix
children.text: State representatives of nonmotorized users; and

children.label: x
children.text: other major Federal, State, tribal, and local safety stakeholders;

children.label: B
children.text: analyzes and makes effective use of State, regional, local, or tribal safety data;

children.label: C
children.text: addresses engineering, management, operation, education, enforcement, and emergency services elements (including integrated, interoperable emergency communications) of highway safety as key factors in evaluating highway projects;

children.label: D
children.text: considers safety needs of, and high-fatality segments of, all public roads, including non-State-owned public roads and roads on tribal land;

children.label: E
children.text: considers the results of State, regional, or local transportation and highway safety planning processes;

children.label: F
children.text: describes a program of strategies to reduce or eliminate safety hazards;

children.label: G
children.text: includes a vulnerable road user safety assessment;

children.label: H
children.text: is approved by the Governor of the State or a responsible State agency;

children.label: I
children.text: is consistent with section 135(g); and

children.label: J
children.text: is updated and submitted to the Secretary for approval as required under subsection (d)(2).

children.heading: .—Systemic safety improvement
children.label: 14 .—Systemic safety improvement
children.text: The term “systemic safety improvement” means an improvement that is widely implemented based on high-risk roadway features that are correlated with particular crash types, rather than crash frequency.

children.label: A
children.text: with a fatality analysis reporting system person attribute code that is included in the definition of the term “number of non-motorized fatalities” in section 490.205 of title 23, Code of Federal Regulations (or successor regulations); or

children.label: B
children.text: described in the term “number of non-motorized serious injuries” in that section.

children.heading: .—Vulnerable road user safety assessment
children.label: 16 .—Vulnerable road user safety assessment
children.text: The term “vulnerable road user safety assessment” means an assessment of the safety performance of the State with respect to vulnerable road users and the plan of the State to improve the safety of vulnerable road users as described in subsection ().l

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: The Secretary shall carry out a highway safety improvement program.

children.heading: .—Purpose
children.label: 2 .—Purpose
children.text: The purpose of the highway safety improvement program shall be to achieve a significant reduction in traffic fatalities and serious injuries on all public roads, including non-State-owned public roads and roads on tribal land.

children.label: A
children.text: develops, implements, and updates a State strategic highway safety plan that identifies and analyzes highway safety problems and opportunities as provided in subsections (a)(13) and (d);

children.label: B
children.text: produces a program of projects or strategies to reduce identified safety problems; and

children.label: C
children.text: evaluates the strategic highway safety plan on a regularly recurring basis in accordance with subsection (d)(1) to ensure the accuracy of the data and priority of proposed strategies.

children.heading: .—Identification and analysis of highway safety problems and opportunities
children.label: 2 .—Identification and analysis of highway safety problems and opportunities
i
children.text: to improve the timeliness, accuracy, completeness, uniformity, integration, and accessibility of the safety data on all public roads, including non-State-owned public roads and roads on tribal land in the State;

children.label: ii
children.text: to evaluate the effectiveness of data improvement efforts;

children.label: iii
children.text: to link State data systems, including traffic records, with other data systems within the State;

children.label: iv
children.text: to improve the compatibility and interoperability of safety data with other State transportation-related data systems and the compatibility and interoperability of State safety data systems with data systems of other States and national data systems;

children.label: v
children.text: to enhance the ability of the Secretary to observe and analyze national trends in crash occurrences, rates, outcomes, and circumstances; and

children.label: vi
children.text: to improve the collection of data on nonmotorized crashes and to differentiate the safety data for vulnerable road users, including bicyclists, motorcyclists, and pedestrians, from other road users;

children.label: i
children.text: identify hazardous locations, sections, and elements (including roadside obstacles, railway-highway crossing needs, and unmarked or poorly marked roads) that constitute a danger to motorists, vulnerable road users (including motorcyclists, bicyclists, pedestrians), and other highway users;

children.label: ii
children.text: using such criteria as the State determines to be appropriate, establish the relative severity of those locations, in terms of crashes (including crash rates), fatalities, serious injuries, traffic volume levels, and other relevant data;

children.label: iii
children.text: identify the number of fatalities and serious injuries on all public roads by location in the State;

children.label: iv
children.text: identify highway safety improvement projects on the basis of crash experience, crash potential, crash rate, or other data-supported means; and

children.label: v
children.text: consider which projects maximize opportunities to advance safety;

children.label: i
children.text: address traffic safety, including behavioral and infrastructure problems and opportunities on all public roads;

children.label: ii
children.text: focus resources on areas of greatest need; and

children.label: iii
children.text: are coordinated with other State highway safety programs;

children.label: i
children.text: complements the State highway safety program under chapter 4 and the commercial vehicle safety plan under ;section 31102 of title 49

children.label: ii
children.text: includes all public roads, including public non-State-owned roads and roads on tribal land;

children.label: iii
children.text: identifies hazardous locations, sections, and elements on all public roads that constitute a danger to motorists (including motorcyclists), bicyclists, pedestrians, persons with disabilities, and other highway users;

children.label: iv
children.text: includes a means of identifying the relative severity of hazardous locations described in clause (iii) in terms of crashes (including crash rate), serious injuries, fatalities, and traffic volume levels;

children.label: v
children.text: improves the ability of the State to identify the number of fatalities and serious injuries on all public roads in the State with a breakdown by functional classification and ownership in the State; and

children.label: vi
children.text: improves the ability of the State to differentiate the fatalities and serious injuries of vulnerable road users, including bicyclists, motorcyclists, and pedestrians, from other road users;

children.label: i
children.text: determine priorities for the correction of hazardous road locations, sections, and elements (including railway-highway crossing improvements), as identified through safety data analysis;

children.label: ii
children.text: identify opportunities for preventing the development of such hazardous conditions; and

children.label: iii
children.text: establish and implement a schedule of highway safety improvement projects for hazard correction and hazard prevention; and

children.label: i
children.text: establish an evaluation process to analyze and assess results achieved by highway safety improvement projects carried out in accordance with procedures and criteria established by this section; and

children.label: ii
children.text: use the information obtained under clause (i) in setting priorities for highway safety improvement projects.

children.heading: Updates to Strategic Highway Safety Plans.—
children.label: d Updates to Strategic Highway Safety Plans.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: Not later than 1 year after the date of enactment of the MAP–21, the Secretary shall establish requirements for regularly recurring State updates of strategic highway safety plans.

children.heading: .—Contents of updated strategic highway safety plans
children.label: B .—Contents of updated strategic highway safety plans
i
children.text: the findings of road safety audits;

children.label: ii
children.text: the locations of fatalities and serious injuries;

children.label: iii
children.text: the locations that do not have an empirical history of fatalities and serious injuries, but possess risk factors for potential crashes;

children.label: iv
children.text: rural roads, including all public roads, commensurate with fatality data;

children.label: v
children.text: motor vehicle crashes that include fatalities or serious injuries to pedestrians and bicyclists;

children.label: vi
children.text: the cost-effectiveness of improvements;

children.label: vii
children.text: improvements to rail-highway grade crossings; and

children.label: viii
children.text: safety on all public roads, including non-State-owned public roads and roads on tribal land.

children.heading: Approval of updated strategic highway safety plans.—
children.label: 2 Approval of updated strategic highway safety plans.—
i
children.text: update the strategic highway safety plans of the State in accordance with the requirements established by the Secretary under this subsection; and

children.label: ii
children.text: submit the updated plans to the Secretary, along with a detailed description of the process used to update the plan.

children.label: i
children.text: the updated strategic highway safety plan is consistent with the requirements of this subsection and subsection (a)(13); and

children.label: ii
children.text: the process used is consistent with the requirements of this subsection.

children.heading: .—Penalty for failure to have an approved updated strategic highway safety plan
children.label: 3 .—Penalty for failure to have an approved updated strategic highway safety plan
children.text: If a State does not have an updated strategic highway safety plan with a process approved by the Secretary by August 1 of the fiscal year beginning after the date of establishment of the requirements under paragraph (1), the State shall not be eligible to receive any additional limitation pursuant to the redistribution of the limitation on obligations for Federal-aid highway and highway safety construction programs that occurs after August 1 for each succeeding fiscal year until the fiscal year during which the plan is approved.

children.label: A
children.text: any highway safety improvement project on any public road or publicly owned bicycle or pedestrian pathway or trail;

children.label: B
children.text: as provided in subsection (g); or

children.label: C
children.text: any project to maintain minimum levels of retroreflectivity with respect to a public road, without regard to whether the project is included in an applicable State strategic highway safety plan.

children.heading: Use of other funding for safety.—
children.label: 2 Use of other funding for safety.—

children.heading: .—Effect of section
children.label: A .—Effect of section
children.text: Nothing in this section prohibits the use of funds made available under other provisions of this title for highway safety improvement projects.

children.heading: .—Use of other funds
children.label: B .—Use of other funds
children.text: States are encouraged to address the full scope of the safety needs and opportunities of the States by using funds made available under other provisions of this title (except a provision that specifically prohibits that use).

children.heading: Flexible funding for specified safety projects.—
children.label: 3 Flexible funding for specified safety projects.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: To advance the implementation of a State strategic highway safety plan, a State may use not more than 10 percent of the amounts apportioned to the State under section 104(b)(3) for a fiscal year to carry out specified safety projects.

children.heading: .—Rule of construction
children.label: B .—Rule of construction
children.text: Nothing in this paragraph requires a State to revise any State process, plan, or program in effect on the date of enactment of this paragraph.

children.heading: .—Requirements
children.label: i .—Requirements
children.text: A project carried out under this paragraph shall be subject to all requirements under this section that apply to a highway safety improvement project.

children.heading: .—Other apportioned programs
children.label: ii .—Other apportioned programs
children.text: Nothing in this paragraph prohibits the use of funds made available under other provisions of this title for a specified safety project that is a noninfrastructure project.

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: The term “data improvement activities” means a project or activity to further the capacity of a State to make more informed and effective safety infrastructure investment decisions.

children.label: i
children.text: to create, update, or enhance a highway basemap of all public roads in a State;

children.label: ii
children.text: to collect safety data, including data identified as part of the model inventory for roadway elements, for creation of or use on a highway basemap of all public roads in a State;

children.label: iii
children.text: to store and maintain safety data in an electronic manner;

children.label: iv
children.text: to develop analytical processes for safety data elements;

children.label: v
children.text: to acquire and implement roadway safety analysis tools; and

children.label: vi
children.text: to support the collection, maintenance, and sharing of safety data on all public roads and related systems associated with the analytical usage of that data.

children.label: A
children.text: establish a subset of the model inventory of roadway elements that are useful for the inventory of roadway safety; and

children.label: B
children.text: ensure that States adopt and use the subset to improve data collection.

children.heading: .—High-risk rural road safety
children.label: 1 .—High-risk rural road safety
children.text: If the fatality rate on rural roads in a State increases over the most recent 2-year period for which data are available, that State shall be required to obligate in the next fiscal year for projects on high risk rural roads an amount equal to at least 200 percent of the amount of funds the State received for fiscal year 2009 for high risk rural roads under subsection (f) of this section, as in effect on the day before the date of enactment of the MAP–21.

children.heading: .—Older drivers
children.label: 2 .—Older drivers
children.text: If traffic fatalities and serious injuries per capita for drivers and pedestrians over the age of 65 in a State increases during the most recent 2-year period for which data are available, that State shall be required to include, in the subsequent Strategic Highway Safety Plan of the State, strategies to address the increases in those rates, taking into account the recommendations included in the publication of the Federal Highway Administration entitled “Highway Design Handbook for Older Drivers and Pedestrians” (FHWA–RD–01–103), and dated May 2001, or as subsequently revised and updated.

children.heading: .—Vulnerable road user safety
children.label: 3 .—Vulnerable road user safety
children.text: If the total annual fatalities of vulnerable road users in a State represents not less than 15 percent of the total annual crash fatalities in the State, that State shall be required to obligate not less than 15 percent of the amounts apportioned to the State under section 104(b)(3) for the following fiscal year for highway safety improvement projects to address the safety of vulnerable road users.

children.label: A
children.text: describes progress being made to implement highway safety improvement projects under this section;

children.label: B
children.text: assesses the effectiveness of those improvements; and

children.label: i
children.text: the number and rate of fatalities on all public roads with, to the maximum extent practicable, a breakdown by functional classification and ownership in the State;

children.label: ii
children.text: the number and rate of serious injuries on all public roads with, to the maximum extent practicable, a breakdown by functional classification and ownership in the State; and

children.label: iii
children.text: the occurrences of fatalities and serious injuries at railway-highway crossings.

children.heading: .—Contents; schedule
children.label: 2 .—Contents; schedule
children.text: The Secretary shall establish the content and schedule for the submission of the report under paragraph (1).

children.label: A
children.text: the website of the Department; and

children.label: B
children.text: such other means as the Secretary determines to be appropriate.

children.heading: .—Discovery and admission into evidence of certain reports, surveys, and information
children.label: 4 .—Discovery and admission into evidence of certain reports, surveys, and information
children.text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, reports, surveys, schedules, lists, or data compiled or collected for any purpose relating to this section, shall not be subject to discovery or admitted into evidence in a Federal or State court proceeding or considered for other purposes in any action for damages arising from any occurrence at a location identified or addressed in the reports, surveys, schedules, lists, or other data.

use obligation authority equal to the apportionment of the State for the prior year under section 104(b)(3) only for highway safety improvement projects under this section until the Secretary determines that the State has met or made significant progress toward meeting the safety performance targets of the State; and

children.label: A
children.text: identifies roadway features that constitute a hazard to road users;

children.label: B
children.text: identifies highway safety improvement projects on the basis of crash experience, crash potential, or other data-supported means;

children.label: C
children.text: describes how highway safety improvement program funds will be allocated, including projects, activities, and strategies to be implemented;

children.label: D
children.text: describes how the proposed projects, activities, and strategies funded under the State highway safety improvement program will allow the State to make progress toward achieving the safety performance targets of the State; and

children.label: E
children.text: describes the actions the State will undertake to meet the safety performance targets of the State.

children.heading: .—Federal Share of Highway Safety Improvement Projects
children.label: j .—Federal Share of Highway Safety Improvement Projects
children.text: Except as provided in sections 120 and 130, the Federal share of the cost of a highway safety improvement project carried out with funds apportioned to a State under section 104(b)(3) shall be 90 percent.

children.heading: Data Collection on Unpaved Public Roads.—
children.label: k Data Collection on Unpaved Public Roads.—
A
children.text: the State does not use funds provided to carry out this section for a project on any such roads until the State completes a collection of the required model inventory of roadway elements for the applicable road segment; and

children.label: B
children.text: the State demonstrates that the State consulted with affected Indian tribes before ceasing to collect data with respect to such roads that are included in the National Tribal Transportation Facility Inventory under .section 202(b)(1) of this title

children.heading: .—Rule of construction
children.label: 2 .—Rule of construction
children.text: Nothing in this subsection may be construed to allow a State to cease data collection related to serious injuries or fatalities.

children.heading: Vulnerable Road User Safety Assessment.—
children.label: l Vulnerable Road User Safety Assessment.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: Not later than 2 years after the date of enactment of this subsection, each State shall complete a vulnerable road user safety assessment.

children.label: i
children.text: includes data such as location, roadway functional classification, design speed, speed limit, and time of day;

children.label: ii
children.text: considers the demographics of the locations of fatalities and serious injuries, including race, ethnicity, income, and age; and

children.label: iii
children.text: based on the data, identifies areas as “high-risk” to vulnerable road users; and

children.label: B
children.text: a program of projects or strategies to reduce safety risks to vulnerable road users in areas identified as high-risk under subparagraph (A)(iii).

children.heading: .—Use of data
children.label: 3 .—Use of data
children.text: In carrying out a vulnerable road user safety assessment under paragraph (1), a State shall use data from the most recent 5-year period for which data is available.

children.label: A
children.text: take into consideration a safe system approach; and

children.label: B
children.text: consult with local governments, metropolitan planning organizations, and regional transportation planning organizations that represent a high-risk area identified under paragraph (2)(A)(iii).

children.heading: .—Update
children.label: 5 .—Update
children.text: A State shall update the vulnerable road user safety assessment of the State in accordance with the updates required to the State strategic highway safety plan under subsection (d).

children.heading: .—Requirement for transportation system access
children.label: 6 .—Requirement for transportation system access
children.text: The program of projects developed under paragraph (2)(B) may not degrade transportation system access for vulnerable road users.

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: Not later than 1 year after the date of enactment of this subsection, the Secretary shall develop guidance for States to carry out this subsection.

children.heading: .—Consultation
children.label: B .—Consultation
children.text: In developing the guidance under this paragraph, the Secretary shall consult with the States and relevant safety stakeholders.
